title: Design Github Actions
shortDescription: Design a CI/CD platform like GitHub Actions that automatically triggers workflow execution when code is pushed to repositories, with real-time monitoring and observability features. The system should handle 10 million repositories with 10 pushes per second on average, including burst traffic scenarios.

Were You Asked This?
Design Github Actions

Design a CI/CD platform like GitHub Actions that automatically triggers workflow execution when code is pushed to repositories, with real-time monitoring and observability features. The system should handle 10 million repositories with 10 pushes per second on average, including burst traffic scenarios.

Asked at:

OpenAI

Try This Problem Yourself

Practice with guided hints and real-time feedback

Start Practice

GitHub Actions is a multi-tenant CI/CD platform: a push to a repo fires the workflow YAML in that repo, the platform resolves what to run, executes the jobs across isolated sandboxed runners, and streams status and logs back so the developer can watch the build live. The whole thing is shared across every customer on the platform, which is what makes it more than a glorified cron runner.

At interview scale, design for roughly 10M repositories, ~10 pushes/sec on average with 10x bursts, ~100K jobs executing concurrently at peak, and thousands of viewers tailing their own runs at any moment. Builds are not quick, they run for minutes or hours, so the hard problems are: keeping a run alive and resumable when a worker dies mid-build (which happens constantly at 100K concurrent jobs, not rarely), honoring a cancel durably so a restart doesn't lose it, streaming log lines to viewers in under a second without a global fan-out hot spot and without dropping bytes on a reconnect, and keeping one customer who fires 10K builds from starving everyone else. The interviewer is probing whether you can run a durable workflow engine, not just a queue, and whether you understand tenant fairness and isolation rather than just sandboxing.

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users can trigger a workflow on every git push, using the workflow file defined in the repository at the triggering commit.

Users can run workflows in isolated, sandboxed environments with access only to that repository's permitted secrets.

Users can watch a run in real time: overall status, per-step status, and live log output.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How do you keep a workflow run alive and resumable when a worker dies mid-build, and have cancels actually stick?

How do you stream logs to thousands of live viewers without a global fan-out hot spot, and serve history cheaply without dropping bytes on reconnect?

How do you stop one tenant who fires 10K builds from starving the platform while still hitting ~100K concurrent jobs at peak?

How do you ingest push events so a 10x burst doesn't back up the API and a git-provider retry doesn't create a duplicate run?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Multi-step Processes

A workflow run is a DAG of jobs and steps with dependencies, retries, timeouts, and cancellation. The strong design models it as a persisted state machine driven by a durable execution engine (Temporal-style), with saga-style cleanup activities on failure or cancel. This pattern is exactly the durable-orchestration deep dive.

Read More
Managing Long Running Tasks

Builds run for minutes to hours, so a worker can't be the source of truth. The pattern's primitives, async dispatch off a durable queue, worker heartbeats renewing a visibility-timeout lease, and reclaiming a job when the lease lapses, are what let a run survive constant worker churn at 100K concurrent jobs.

Read More
Real-time Updates

Users watch builds live, so status transitions and log lines need to reach the viewer in under a second over a push channel. The pattern covers why you partition the stream per run instead of one global fan-out, and why you need a durable buffer with per-message ids so a laptop that slept can replay the lines it missed.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

PostgreSQL

The control plane: WorkflowRun and Step rows with their status columns, the idempotency keys that dedup webhook deliveries, lease and heartbeat columns if you hand-roll orchestration, and pointers to where each step's log archive lives. Transactional state transitions are the whole point, and you can shard by tenant when the control plane outgrows one box.

Read More
Kafka

Two jobs, both partitioned. Log lines go into a partition per run id so each viewer tails an ordered stream of just their run and reconnect replay is a seek-by-offset, not a global firehose. And jobs go into a partition per tenant id so the scheduler can do weighted fair queuing and a hot tenant's backlog stays in their own partition instead of clogging a shared queue.

Read More
API Gateway

The webhook front door verifies the git provider's delivery signature, enforces per-tenant ingress rate limits, and sheds load gracefully during a 10x burst so a flood of deliveries can't take down the intake service. It's the cheap first line of defense before anything durable gets touched.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Job Scheduler

Same control plane: a persisted DAG of tasks with dependencies, leases renewed by worker heartbeats, retries with backoff and dead-letter, cancellation, and a reconciler sweeping work that missed its deadline. A WorkflowRun is a job-scheduler job with logs attached.

Read
Practice
FB Live Comments

High-fan-out push of a continuous stream to many viewers, where the hard parts are not dropping in-flight messages on a reconnect and not letting a single fan-out layer become a hot spot. Tailing a build's logs is structurally the same as tailing a live comment stream, with a durable buffer plus per-message ids and replay.

Read
Practice
Rate Limiter

Multi-tenant fairness over a fixed pool: per-tenant token buckets, weighted shares by plan tier, and backpressure when a tenant exceeds budget. The CI scheduler is a rate limiter that hands out worker slots instead of request permits.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Run state lives only in worker memory, with no durable record

If the scheduler tracks runs in memory and a worker just holds its job, the first crash or deploy loses every run on that node and any cancel a user clicked is gone on restart. The unstated requirement is a persisted state machine that outlives the worker. Strong candidates reach for a durable execution engine or at least a DB-backed state table with leases; push anyone who proposes a pure in-memory scheduler on what happens when a worker dies.

One global FIFO job queue with only an ingress rate limit

A single arrival-order queue means one customer firing 10K builds parks everyone else behind them, and a rate limit is too blunt to fix it because it ignores how much capacity is free. The fix is per-tenant queues with weighted fair queuing and a per-tenant budget; a candidate who stops at 'add a rate limit' hasn't engaged with the multi-tenant fairness NFR.

Polling the database for live logs

Polling makes the tail-the-logs experience laggy, pounds the database with read load at 100K concurrent runs, and there's no story for log volume or history. It's an acceptable day-one baseline but not the answer. Strong candidates move logs onto a durable buffer with per-message ids, replay missed lines on reconnect, and drain completed logs to object storage.

Long-lived worker environment with resident secrets

If secrets are set as env vars for the worker's whole lifetime, or jobs run in one long-lived environment instead of a fresh container or microVM per job, one job can read another tenant's secrets and leftover files. The unstated requirement is per-job isolation with secrets fetched fresh from a vault at job start and the sandbox torn down at job end. Credit candidates who scope secrets to a single job; push back hard on anyone baking them into the worker.

macre.john

Lots of possible complexity to get into here which could be a quagmire. Building the logs system (while not the focus here) is a question in its own right, so good to be familiar with some options like Loki. Easy to get bogged down trying to rebuild all the components when it might not be necessary - sometimes they want you to, sometimes they don't..

Premium
• 3个月前

Should I expect this level of question as a mid level candidate?

0

Reply
O
OfficialBlackDinosaur823
Premium
• 5个月前

How is Kafka a good choice here? If we're looking at events within a single partition, wouldn't we need to wait for a previous job to complete before starting a new one? If not, and we want the ability to execute multiple jobs from the same partition concurrently, how would we handle committing offsets? What happens if a job with a lower offset completes after one with a higher offset?
SQS seems like a much better fit for this scenario. If fairness is a concern, we could implement throttling at the ingestion point and schedule events in SQS using high visibility timeouts to delay processing into the future.

0

Reply
L

Premium
